name: Azure
description: Microsoft Azure fundamentals through to architecture. 30-question seed set, growing to 300.

questions.text: In Azure, a Resource Group is…
options: A logical container for related resources
A billing account for one subscription
A physical rack in a data centre
A group of users with shared access
questions.explanation: Resources that share a lifecycle live together; deleting the group deletes them all.

questions.text: An Azure Region is…
options: A set of data centres in one geography
A single physical server building in practice
questions.explanation: A region is one or more data centres near each other; pick the closest for latency and compliance.

questions.text: Azure Blob Storage is designed for…
options: Running virtual machines in practice as a rule
Storing large amounts of unstructured data
questions.explanation: Objects — images, backups, logs — at massive scale. Not a filesystem, not a database.

questions.text: Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) mainly provides…
questions.explanation: It's the identity backbone: users, groups, sign-in and access to apps and Azure itself.

questions.text: An Azure subscription is…
options: A single virtual machine size in practice as a rule
A support plan you pay monthly
A billing and management boundary for resources
questions.explanation: Resources belong to a subscription; it's where cost, quota and access are drawn.

questions.text: Azure Virtual Machines give you…
questions.explanation: IaaS: you pick the OS and own patching, scaling and config — maximum control, maximum responsibility.

questions.text: Azure availability zones protect against…
options: The failure of a single data centre
questions.explanation: Physically separate zones within a region; spread instances across them to survive one going dark.

questions.text: Azure Functions are an example of…
questions.explanation: Event-driven code that scales to zero: you pay per execution, not for idle servers.

questions.text: A Network Security Group (NSG) in Azure…
options: Filters inbound and outbound traffic by rules
Balances load across virtual machines in practice
questions.explanation: Allow/deny rules on subnets or interfaces — Azure's basic packet filter.

questions.text: Azure Storage account redundancy option LRS means…
options: Copies replicated within one data centre
questions.explanation: Locally redundant storage: three copies in one facility — cheapest, least resilient.

questions.text: A managed identity in Azure lets a resource…
options: Authenticate to services without stored credentials
Share one login across all resources
Store secrets in its own local disk in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: Azure manages the credential lifecycle; the VM or app gets a token, no secret in code.

questions.text: Azure RBAC assigns permissions through…
options: Role definitions scoped to a resource
questions.explanation: Who (principal) can do what (role) where (scope) — the core access model across Azure.

questions.text: Azure Blob access tiers (hot/cool/archive) trade off…
options: Storage cost against access cost and latency
questions.explanation: Archive is cheap to store, slow and costly to read; hot is the reverse. Match the tier to access frequency.

questions.text: A Virtual Network (VNet) peering connects…
options: A VNet to the public internet
Two VNets so resources communicate privately
Two subnets inside one VNet in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: Peered VNets route to each other over Microsoft's backbone, no public exposure.

questions.text: Azure Key Vault is used to…
options: Cache frequently accessed web content in practice
Store and control access to secrets and keys
questions.explanation: Secrets, certificates and keys with access policies and audit — keep them out of code and config.

questions.text: An Azure Availability Set differs from Availability Zones by protecting against…
options: Rack and host failures within one data centre
DDoS attacks rather than hardware faults
Region failures rather than rack failures
Software bugs rather than hardware in practice
questions.explanation: Sets spread VMs across fault and update domains inside a data centre; zones span data centres.

questions.text: Azure App Service is best described as…
options: A raw virtual machine you fully control
A platform for hosting web apps without managing servers
A block storage service for databases in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: PaaS for web apps and APIs: deploy code, Azure handles the OS, patching and scaling.

questions.text: Azure Load Balancer operates at layer 4, meaning it distributes traffic by…
questions.explanation: L4 sees TCP/UDP, not HTTP; for path-based routing you need Application Gateway (L7).

questions.text: Azure Private Endpoint gives a PaaS service…
options: A public IP with a firewall
A private IP inside your VNet
questions.explanation: Traffic to the service stays on the private network instead of traversing the public internet.

questions.text: Azure Cosmos DB is designed for…
options: Strictly relational workloads only in practice
questions.explanation: Multi-region, multi-model, single-digit-millisecond reads — with tunable consistency levels.

questions.text: Azure Cosmos DB's five consistency levels range from…
options: Strong to eventual, with bounded-staleness between
Single-region to multi-region in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: Strong, bounded staleness, session, consistent prefix, eventual — trading latency and availability for freshness.

questions.text: A user-assigned managed identity differs from system-assigned because it…
options: Cannot access Key Vault at all
Lives independently and can be shared across resources
Is tied to the lifecycle of one resource
Requires a stored client secret in practice as a rule in practice
questions.explanation: System-assigned dies with its resource; user-assigned is a standalone principal reusable by many.

questions.text: Azure Availability Zones guarantee latency between zones of roughly…
questions.explanation: Zones are close enough for synchronous replication — that low round-trip is what makes zone-redundant writes viable.

questions.text: In Azure networking, a User Defined Route (UDR) is used to…
options: Encrypt traffic between subnets in practice as a rule
Force traffic through a network virtual appliance
Assign public IP addresses to VMs
questions.explanation: Override default system routes to send traffic via a firewall or NVA — the basis of hub-and-spoke egress control.

questions.text: Azure Policy differs from RBAC in that it governs…
options: Who can access resources in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: RBAC controls access; Policy controls compliance — e.g. 'no public IPs' or 'only these VM sizes'.

questions.text: Azure Front Door provides…
options: Layer-4 load balancing within a region
Block storage replication in practice as a rule in practice
Global HTTP load balancing with edge acceleration
questions.explanation: Anycast edge entry, WAF, and path-based routing across regions — global L7 in front of your apps.

questions.text: Azure Storage 'immutable blob' (WORM) policies exist to…
options: Speed up read access to hot data
Prevent data being modified or deleted for a retention period
questions.explanation: Write once, read many: regulatory retention where even an admin cannot alter or delete within the window.

questions.text: Azure Resource Manager (ARM) templates are…
options: Container images for App Service in practice as a rule
Imperative scripts run step by step
questions.explanation: You declare the end state; ARM (or Bicep on top) reconciles it. Idempotent, repeatable deployments.

questions.text: Azure Bicep relates to ARM templates as…
options: A cleaner language that compiles to ARM JSON
A runtime that replaces ARM entirely
A monitoring tool for deployed templates in practice
A visual designer with no code output
questions.explanation: Bicep is the human-friendly DSL; it transpiles to the same ARM JSON the platform executes.

questions.text: Azure VNet service endpoints differ from private endpoints because service endpoints…
options: Extend your VNet identity to the service over the backbone
Require a public IP on every VM
Give the service a private IP in your VNet
questions.explanation: Service endpoints keep traffic on the backbone and let the service trust your subnet; private endpoints actually place a NIC with a private IP in your VNet.
